nodes.title: Normalize path into a segment array
nodes.why: Callers can pass either "a.b.c" or ["a", "b", "c"]. Normalizing early keeps the rest of the function simple.
nodes.tradeoff: A little extra setup code, but much friendlier API.

nodes.title: Detect numeric array indexes explicitly
nodes.why: This avoids accidentally treating non-index strings as array indexes.
nodes.tradeoff: Regex is stricter and safer, but slightly less obvious at first glance.

nodes.title: Handle invalid and empty paths up front

anchor.snippet: if (!segs) throw new TypeError('setByPath: path must be a string or array');

nodes.why: Invalid paths fail clearly, and empty paths return the new root value immediately.
nodes.alternative: Silently ignore invalid input and return the original object.
nodes.tradeoff: Stricter behavior, but easier debugging and clearer contracts.

nodes.title: Create object or array based on the current segment
nodes.why: When intermediate data is missing or primitive, this picks the right container type so the path can continue.
nodes.alternative: Always create objects for missing branches.
nodes.tradeoff: Needs index detection each step, but supports mixed paths like a.b.0.c correctly.

nodes.title: Clone each level before writing
nodes.why: This keeps updates immutable, so the original input is never mutated.
nodes.alternative: Write directly into the original object/array.
nodes.tradeoff: Creates new objects along the path, but gives predictable state updates.

nodes.title: Recursively rebuild only the touched branch
nodes.why: Only nodes on the updated path are recreated; untouched branches keep their references.
nodes.alternative: Deep-clone the entire object first, then set the value.
nodes.tradeoff: Recursion is a bit more complex, but much more efficient for large objects.
